sentences.base_language: I have a cold and I go to the pharmacy.
sentences.target_language: 風邪をひいたので、薬局に行きます。
sentences.romanized: Kaze wo hiita no de, yakkyoku ni ikimasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 風邪をひく (kaze wo hiku) is the set expression for catching a cold. の で (no de) gives a reason: 〜ので、〜 (because ~, ~). It is softer than から (kara) and often feels more natural in everyday speech.

sentences.base_language: My throat hurts and I have a runny nose.
sentences.target_language: 喉が痛いし、鼻水も出ています。
sentences.romanized: Nodo ga itai shi, hanamizu mo dete imasu.
sentences.literal: Throat hurts and, runny-nose also coming-out is.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜し (shi) lists multiple reasons or characteristics: 喉が痛いし、鼻水も出ている (my throat hurts, and on top of that I have a runny nose). It is softer and less exhaustive than the て-form and suggests there may be even more reasons.

sentences.target_language: 薬剤師さんに話しかけます。
sentences.grammarNote:
**薬剤師** (yakuzaishi) = pharmacist: **薬** (yaku, medicine) + **剤** (zai, preparation/compound) + **師** (shi, expert). The **さん** suffix adds polite respect.
**話しかける** (hanashikakeru) = 'to speak to / address someone': **話す** (hanasu, speak) + **かける** (kakeru, to start/apply). More nuanced than just **話す** — implies initiating the conversation.
**に** marks the person being addressed.

sentences.base_language: I say, 'My throat hurts and I have a fever.'
sentences.target_language: 「喉が痛いし、熱もあります」と伝えます。
sentences.romanized: 'Nodo ga itai shi, netsu mo arimasu' to tsutaemasu.
sentences.literal: 'Throat hurts and, fever also exists' [quote] convey.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜が痛いです (〜ga itai desu) uses the body part as the grammatical subject with が, and 痛い as a descriptive adjective: 喉が痛い (my throat hurts), 頭が痛い (my head hurts). The pattern is: [body part] + が + 痛い.

sentences.base_language: The pharmacist asks, 'Do you also have a cough?'
sentences.target_language: 「咳も出ていますか？」と聞きます。
sentences.romanized: 'Seki mo dete imasu ka?' to kikimasu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**咳** (seki) = cough. **咳が出る** (seki ga deru) = 'to have a cough' — literally 'a cough comes out'. Japanese uses **出る** (deru, to come out) for bodily symptoms: **鼻水が出る** (runny nose), **熱が出る** (develop a fever).
**も** (mo) = 'also/too': **咳も** = 'cough too (in addition to the other symptoms)'.
The **と聞きます** construction = '[someone] asks, saying [quote]'.

sentences.target_language: 「少し出ています」と答えます。
sentences.grammarNote:
**少し** (sukoshi) = a little/a bit. Casual: **ちょっと** (chotto), also used to soften a refusal.
**出ています** (dete imasu) = 'is coming out' (ongoing state): **出る** (deru) → te-form **出て** + **います**.
**答えます** (kotaemasu) = answers/responds (polite present): **答える** (kotaeru, to answer) → **答えます**. Compare: **言います** (iimasu, says) vs **答えます** (answers, implying a response to a question).

sentences.base_language: The pharmacist recommends a cold medicine.
sentences.target_language: 薬剤師さんが風邪薬を勧めてくれます。
sentences.romanized: Yakuzaishi-san ga kazegusuri wo susumete kuremasu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**風邪薬** (kazegusuri) = cold medicine: **風邪** (kaze, cold) + **薬** (kusuri, medicine).
**勧めてくれます** (susumete kuremasu) = 'recommends for me': **勧める** (susumeru, to recommend) + **くれる** (kureru, to give/do for the speaker). The **くれる** marks that this action benefits the speaker — a key feature of Japanese giving verbs.
**が** marks the pharmacist as the doer (subject doing the action for me).

sentences.base_language: I ask, 'Is it okay to take this with food?'
sentences.target_language: 「食後に飲んでもいいですか？」と聞きます。
sentences.romanized: 'Shokugo ni nonde mo ii desu ka?' to kikimasu.
sentences.literal: 'After-meals at take even good is?' [quote] ask.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜てもいいですか (〜te mo ii desu ka) is the standard way to ask for permission: 'Is it okay to ~?' The answer 〜てもいいです means 'yes, you may'. The negative 〜てはいけません means 'you must not'. This pattern is essential for polite requests.

sentences.base_language: The pharmacist says, 'Please take it after meals, three times a day.'
sentences.target_language: 「一日三回、食後に飲んでください」と言います。
sentences.romanized: 'Ichinichi sankai, shokugo ni nonde kudasai' to iimasu.
sentences.literal: 'One-day three-times, after-meals at take please' [quote] says.
sentences.grammarNote:
**一日三回** (ichinichi sankai) = 'three times a day': **一日** (ichinichi, one day/per day) + **三回** (sankai, three times). Dosage instructions always use this pattern.
**食後** (shokugo) = after meals: **食** (shoku, food/eat) + **後** (go, after). Compare: **食前** (shokuzen, before meals), **食間** (shokukan, between meals).
**飲む** (nomu) = 'to drink/take (medicine)' — the same verb is used for both drinking and swallowing pills.

sentences.base_language: I buy the medicine and thank the pharmacist.
sentences.target_language: 薬を買って、薬剤師さんにお礼を言います。
sentences.romanized: Kusuri wo katte, yakuzaishi-san ni orei wo iimasu.
sentences.literal: Medicine [obj] buy and, pharmacist [to] thanks say.
sentences.grammarNote:
**買って** (katte) = te-form of **買う** (kau, to buy): links two actions — 'buy and [then] thank'.
**お礼を言う** (orei wo iu) = 'to express thanks/gratitude': **お礼** (orei, thanks/gratitude) + **言う** (iu, to say). **お** is an honorific prefix.
Alternative: **ありがとうと言う** = 'say thank you'. **お礼を言う** is slightly more formal and complete.

sentences.base_language: I hope I get better soon.
sentences.target_language: 早く良くなるといいです。
sentences.romanized: Hayaku yoku naru to ii desu.
sentences.literal: Quickly better become if good is.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜といいです** = 'I hope ~ / it would be nice if ~': **と** (conditional) + **いいです** (it's good). Expresses a hopeful wish.
**早く** (hayaku) = 'quickly/soon': adverb form of **早い** (hayai, fast/early). い-adjective → adverb: drop **い**, add **く**.
**良くなる** = 'get better/recover': **良い** (yoi, good) → adverb **よく** + **なる** (become). This **〜くなる** pattern = 'become more [adjective]'.
